title: Number of solutions to equations challenge
description: Given a linear equation with missing values, determine appropriate values in order to have one, zero, or infinite solutions. For example, complete the equation -11x + 4 = __x + __ so that it has no solutions.
topic_path: Number of solutions to equations challenge

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have exactly one solution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have exactly one solution, $\blueD{a}$ must be different from $\maroonD{c}$.

In the following equation, $\blueD{a}$ is different from $\maroonD{c}$. 

* $\blueD2x-31=\maroonD{-2}x-31$


 So, the equation has exactly one solution.

The answer:

* $\blueD2x-31=\maroonD{-2}x-31$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have exactly one solution?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have exactly one solution, $\blueD{a}$ must be different from $\maroonD{c}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is different from $\maroonD{c}$. 

* $\blueD{19}x+18=\maroonD{-19}x+18$
* $\blueD{19}x-18=\maroonD{-19}x+18$


 So, the equations have exactly one solution.

The answer:

* $\blueD{19}x+18=\maroonD{-19}x+18$
* $\blueD{19}x-18=\maroonD{-19}x+18$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have exactly one solution?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have exactly one solution, $\blueD{a}$ must be different from $\maroonD{c}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is different from $\maroonD{c}$. 

* $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{5}x-5$
*  $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{-12}x-12$
*  $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{5}x+12$



 So, the equations have exactly one solution.

The answer:

* $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{5}x-5$
*  $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{-12}x-12$
*  $\blueD{-5}x+12=\maroonD{5}x+12$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have exactly one solution?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have exactly one solution, $\blueD{a}$ must be different from $\maroonD{c}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is different from $\maroonD{c}$. 

* $\blueD{-13}x+12=\maroonD{13}x-13$
* $\blueD{12}x+12=\maroonD{13}x-12$
* $\blueD{-13}x+12=\maroonD{13}x+13$
* $\blueD{12}x+12=\maroonD{13}x+12$


 So, the equations have exactly one solution.

The answer:

* $\blueD{-13}x+12=\maroonD{13}x-13$
* $\blueD{12}x+12=\maroonD{13}x-12$
* $\blueD{-13}x+12=\maroonD{13}x+13$
* $\blueD{12}x+12=\maroonD{13}x+12$

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is different from $\maroonD{c}$. 

* $\blueD{-103}x-6=\maroonD{-6}x-103$

 So, the equations have exactly one solution.

The answer:

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have no solutions?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have no solutions, $\blueD{a}$ must be equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ must be different from $\goldE{d}$.

In the following equation, $\blueD{a}$ is equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ is different from $\goldE{d}$.

* $\blueD{14}x\greenD{+23}=\maroonD{14}x\goldE{-23}$


 So, the equation has  no solutions.

The answer:

* $\blueD{14}x\greenD{+23}=\maroonD{14}x\goldE{-23}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have no solutions?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have no solutions, $\blueD{a}$ must be equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ must be different from $\goldE{d}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ is different from $\goldE{d}$.

*  $\blueD{33}x\greenD{+33}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$
*  $\blueD{33}x\greenD{-25}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$
*   $\blueD{33}x\greenD{-33}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$


 So, the equations have no solutions.

The answer:

*  $\blueD{33}x\greenD{+33}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$
*  $\blueD{33}x\greenD{-25}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$
*   $\blueD{33}x\greenD{-33}=\maroonD{33}x\goldE{+25}$

hints:

* $\blueD{29}x\greenD{+11}=\maroonD{29}x\goldE{-11}$
* $\blueD{29}x\greenD{+29}=\maroonD{29}x\goldE{-11}$
* $\blueD{29}x\greenD{+92}=\maroonD{29}x\goldE{-11}$
* $\blueD{29}x\greenD{-29}=\maroonD{29}x\goldE{-11}$


 So, the equations have  no solutions.

The answer:

* $\blueD{29}x\greenD{+11}=\maroonD{29}x\goldE{-11}$

* $\blueD{-6}x\greenD{+35}=\maroonD{-6}x\goldE{+35}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have infinitely many solutions?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have infinitely many solutions, $\blueD{a}$ must be equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ must be equal to $\goldE{d}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ is equal to $\goldE{d}$.

* $\blueD{-19}x\greenD{-19}=\maroonD{-19}x\goldE{-19}$
* $\blueD{-19}x\greenD{+91}=\maroonD{-19}x\goldE{+91}$

 So, the equations have infinitely many solutions.

* $\blueD{-19}x\greenD{-19}=\maroonD{-19}x\goldE{-19}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following equations have infinitely many solutions?**



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation of the form $\blueD{a}x+\greenD{b}=\maroonD{c}x+\goldE{d}$ has:

- exactly one solution when $\blueD{a}\neq \maroonD{c}$

- no solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}\neq \goldE{d}$

- infinitely many solutions when $\blueD{a}=\maroonD{c}$ and $\greenD{b}=\goldE{d}$
In order for our equation to have infinitely many solutions, $\blueD{a}$ must be equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ must be equal to $\goldE{d}$.

In the following equations, $\blueD{a}$ is equal to $\maroonD{c}$, and $\greenD{b}$ is equal to $\goldE{d}$.

* $\blueD{37}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{37}x\goldE{-37}$
* $x\greenD{-37}=x\goldE{-37}$
* $\blueD{73}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{73}x\goldE{-37}$
* $\blueD{74}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{74}x\goldE{-37}$


 So, the equations have infinitely many solutions.

The answer:

* $\blueD{37}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{37}x\goldE{-37}$
* $x\greenD{-37}=x\goldE{-37}$
* $\blueD{73}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{73}x\goldE{-37}$
* $\blueD{74}x\greenD{-37}=\maroonD{74}x\goldE{-37}$
